rescue_reasoning: Although the sub-statement lacks explicit mitigation outcome tokens, it is an enabling action tied to mitigation through the parent. The parent statement frames the support as enabling implementation of “high-impact clean energy actions,” and the sub-statement operationalizes that by “provid[ing] educational materials and training… so that they understand what resources are available… and are prepared to receive funding.” With climate_screen = explicit_parent, the row remains climate-relevant and consistent with Mitigation enablement rather than being dropped or reclassified.
policy_statement: Provide educational materials and training to local governments/public entities so they understand available resources and are prepared to receive funding.
mechanism_description: The state provides educational materials and training so local governments understand available resources and are prepared to receive funding, which strengthens their capacity to implement climate-related projects.
primary_causal_pathway: Capacity building for implementers → ↑ climate project implementation (policies/investments) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changed system: Local governments’ institutional/administrative capability (knowledge of programs, readiness to apply for/receive/manage funds, project planning and compliance capacity) is increased via training and guidance. (2) Mediating process: Improved capacity reduces informational and administrative barriers, increases successful funding uptake and project execution, and improves performance of implemented measures (e.g., energy efficiency, clean energy procurement, fleet electrification). (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Greater and faster deployment of mitigation actions yields net reductions in GHG emissions relative to baseline.
dominant_pathway_test: By strengthening local government capacity, the policy most importantly enables implementation of emissions-reducing climate projects that lead to lower GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The largest climate effect occurs downstream—capacity building enables implementation of concrete climate actions that reduce emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While capacity building could enable adaptation or resource-efficiency measures, the mechanism as stated is broadly “climate-related projects” with funding readiness; the most common and climate-dominant impact across such enabling programs is increased delivery of mitigation projects (emissions reductions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If the enabled portfolio is primarily adaptation (e.g., resilience planning, flood protection) the dominant effect would be Adaptation; if primarily operational efficiency, it could be Resource Efficiency. Those are treated as policy-specific downstream choices rather than inherent to the capacity-building mechanism.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy matching this mechanism (training/education to prepare locals to access resources and implement climate projects) functions as an implementation enabler whose primary climate impact is through increased execution of mitigation actions in typical climate programs.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the enabled actions are mitigation vs adaptation-heavy, and the specific sub-type (buildings, transport, waste, etc.), must be assigned using policy-level evidence about what projects are actually funded/implemented.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Enabling/capacity-building mechanisms are cross-cutting; classification can flip to Adaptation or Resource Efficiency if the program is explicitly limited to resilience or efficiency outcomes. If the training is purely administrative with no clear linkage to climate project implementation, climate impact may be indirect/uncertain.
key_indicators: "Support capacity-building"; "provide educational materials and training"; "local governments and related public entities"; "understand what resources are available"; "prepared to receive funding"
co_benefits: Improved general administrative capacity and fiscal management for non-climate programs; better compliance and project delivery across public services
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as an incentive-program if paired with dedicated funding/set-asides, but the text specifies training/materials (technical assistance) rather than grants or financial inducements.
